concise:
Ayah 1–11 · linked video: 01. Al-Mu’minun (Ayah 1-11)

Surah Al-Muminun: The Qualities of True Believers

Ayah 1

قَدْ أَفْلَحَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ

The surah opens with a powerful declaration: “The true believers have already attained success.” This follows naturally from the end of Surah Al-Hajj, which concluded with guidance for the believers. While Surah Al-Hajj transitioned from addressing all of humanity to focusing on the believers, Surah Al-Muminun takes the reverse approach—it begins with the true believers and later addresses humanity at large.

The word قَدْ serves as an emphatic particle, indicating something that is already accomplished, a done deal. This is remarkable because we often think of the believers’ success as something that will happen in the future—when they enter Paradise. But Allah ﷻ tells us that if you have truly attained iman, you have already become extremely successful.

The word used for success here—أَفْلَحَ—carries profound agricultural imagery. The root word فَلاَح refers to the farmer’s act of tearing the soil, either at the beginning when planting seeds or at the end during harvest. This connection to farming is significant because a farmer faces an entire year of labor with no guarantee of success. Unlike other professions where payment comes weekly or monthly, the farmer receives one paycheck at the end of the year. Everything must go right—the seed must be good, the weather favorable, no drought, no infestation, the right amount of sun. When harvest season finally arrives and the farmer tears the plants from the ground, it represents complete success from beginning to end.

This is the life of the believer. We are working hard throughout this entire life, and our real compensation comes at the end. But Allah ﷻ says there are people whose harvest is guaranteed. Unlike the farmer who faces uncertainty, the true believers are assured of their eventual success.

Notice also the choice of the word الْمُؤْمِنُونَ—“the true believers”—rather than the more common الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا “those who believed.” This distinction is crucial. آمَنُوا is a verb in the past tense, which is inherently unstable and temporary. Just because someone believed in the past doesn’t guarantee the strength of their faith today or tomorrow. But مُؤْمِنُونَ is a noun, an active participle that carries permanence. It has no past, present, or future tense—it simply is.

When Allah ﷻ uses this noun form, He is describing believers whose iman has reached a certain level of maturity and stability. Yes, they still have ups and downs, but they’ve crossed a minimum threshold that they don’t fall below. To visualize this: some people are like swimmers at sea, sometimes their heads go underwater, sometimes they come up for air. But the mu’minun are like people on a boat—yes, the boat rocks, but they constantly remain above a certain level.

The phrase الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا covers a wide spectrum of Muslims, broadly three categories: hypocrites who claim belief but have diseases in their hearts; regular Muslims who haven’t yet tasted the sweetness of stable iman; and the elite true believers we’re discussing now. Within each category there are ranks and degrees, but for us as observers, we cannot judge who belongs where. When Allah ﷻ says “O you who believe,” we must treat all who say the shahada equally. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that once someone says “As-salamu alaykum” to you, you have no right to judge their faith.

So why does Allah ﷻ describe these qualities if we cannot judge others? So that we can judge ourselves. This passage serves as a checklist for self-examination. We can also make collective judgments about the Ummah’s state. When Allah ﷻ says in Surah Al-Imran, “You will be supreme if you are true believers,” and we see the Muslim world lacking that supremacy, we must reflect that perhaps we haven’t reached the minimum threshold of collective iman yet.

Ayah 2

ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى صَلَاتِهِمْ خَـٰشِعُونَ

Allah ﷻ now begins to describe who these true believers are: “Those who, especially in their prayers, are people of khushu’.” The word الَّذِينَ introduces a description—“who are they? They are the ones who…”

Notice the emphasis: هُمْ—“they in particular,” especially فِى صَلَاتِهِمْ—“in their prayers.” The use of the noun خَـٰشِعُونَ rather than a verb is significant once again. It indicates permanence—they have reached a stage of maturity where khushu’ has become a constant feature of their prayer. For most of us, perhaps wudu is a constant feature, or facing the qiblah, but khushu’ is not yet constant. These believers have made it so.

What is خُشُوع? The word حَشَر in Arabic describes a fear so overwhelming that your muscles become numb, almost paralyzed. Think of an animal that sees a predator and becomes frozen in fear. Or imagine someone crossing the street who suddenly turns and sees a truck bearing down on them—that look of complete awe and powerlessness in their face is khushu’.

The closest we can understand this is to imagine Bani Israel when Allah ﷻ raised the mountain above their heads as a shadow while giving them the Torah. Picture the feeling of having a mountain suspended above you like a cloud—that overwhelming sense of awe and submission is khushu’. The true believer experiences something of this when standing before Allah ﷻ in prayer.

This ayah suggests that while khushu’ is especially present in their prayers, it’s not limited to prayer alone. It may not be constant outside of prayer, but at least in prayer it has become a constant thing. The singular form صَلَاة is used here, referring to the institution of prayer—whenever and wherever they pray, this is their state.

Khushu’ has become such a central concern that scholars throughout Islamic history have written extensively about developing it. Imam Al-Ghazali authored beautiful works on prayer and khushu’. If we don’t yet see this quality constant in ourselves, we must ask: How will I make this a consistent reality in my life? This is the first checkbox—either we can put a check mark next to it or acknowledge that we haven’t met this first description yet and need to work toward it.

Ayah 3

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ ٱللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ

The next quality follows logically from the first: “And those who, especially from vain talk, are ones who turn away.” If someone has truly developed an awe of Allah ﷻ in prayer—having had this powerful meeting with their Lord where they’ve been corrected, where they’ve cried, where they’ve humbled themselves every few hours—it naturally affects what comes after.

Think of a student who comes home with a bad report card. Their mother calls them, takes the report card, and gives them a serious talking to. Tears flow, and they promise themselves, “I will never do this again.” For the next several hours, their head hangs low. Even at lunch, when their mother tells their father what happened, a new wave of emotion hits. The residual effects carry over between these moments of accountability.

This is what the time between prayers should be like. We stand before Allah ﷻ as if being audited, and it’s supposed to have an effect that carries over until the next prayer. Prayer is a combination of joy at its beauty and sadness at our shortcomings. Every time we stand before Allah ﷻ, we acknowledge how far behind we are from where we should be. If we’re truly in that state, then between prayers we won’t have the heart to engage in meaningless activities.

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ ٱللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ—“those who from laghw turn away.” اللَّغْو refers to speech and activities that produce no benefit—neither worldly benefit nor spiritual benefit in the Hereafter. It’s sitting around talking about nothing for hours, running your mouth endlessly. Yes, conversation is natural, especially when young—staying up talking about random things on road trips—but to be constantly engaged in useless talk is different.

When someone calls just to gossip—“Did you see what that person just did? Did you check Facebook?”—that’s constant laghw. These true believers who have developed a relationship of meaningful conversation with Allah ﷻ realize the value of speech, what it means to be able to use our tongues. This awareness permeates all their conversations.

The word مُعْرِضُونَ means they deliberately turn away from such situations. They don’t even passively sit around listening to others engage in meaningless talk. They actively avoid circumstances where they know their time will be wasted.

In our time, laghw takes many forms. Consider someone obsessed with getting three stars on every level of every version of a mobile game, unable to sleep until they find the perfect move. That’s a laghw problem. People sit with their phones and games everywhere—in waiting rooms, in cars, any free moment. Every free moment that could be used for dhikr, for building our Akhirah, is now consumed by our devices.

Here’s a practical application: delete games from your phone. Delete songs from your phone. Every spare second you have, every free moment—headphones go up and your brain tunes out. This is laghw too. These are the first steps in turning away from vain activities.

Ayah 4

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِلزَّكَوٰةِ فَـٰعِلُونَ

“And those who, especially for the purpose of purification, are active doers.” This is not simply about giving zakat as in purifying one’s wealth—notice it doesn’t say يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكَاةَ (they give zakat). Rather, it says لِلزَّكَوٰةِ فَـٰعِلُونَ (for purification, they are doers).

فَاعِلُونَ indicates active engagement in the work of purification. They realize: my personality is dirty. My character has problems. Maybe I have an anger issue. Maybe I have trouble forgiving people or letting go of grudges. Maybe I speak without realizing how offensive I am. Maybe things get to my heart too quickly. Maybe I’m lazy, disrespectful, inconsiderate. Maybe I just want what I want and never thank anyone. Maybe I’m extremely selfish, too addicted to entertainment.

There are so many flaws in our personalities that we need to identify and address. But here’s the critical point: you’ll never even think about these things as problems until the first two qualities happen. You won’t reach this third stage until you’ve cleaned your mind for salah and stopped wasting time in empty talk. That’s when you finally have time to reflect on yourself and what needs to be fixed.

Here’s a practical activity: literally make a list of things you don’t like about yourself that you’re pretty sure Allah ﷻ doesn’t want in you either. Then ask: how do I address these things? Make a project out of yourself. وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِلزَّكَوٰةِ فَـٰعِلُونَ—they actively work on cleansing themselves.

Our personality is like an onion—it has layers. When you clean one layer and address one problem, you discover underneath it there’s another problem you didn’t even see before. But now that you’ve addressed the first thing, the second becomes obvious. You become more sensitive to issues you weren’t even aware of previously. When you start making yourself a better person, your sensitivity to what it means to be a more refined person increases over time.

For example, regarding modesty: at first, maybe rated R movies seem acceptable—just some bad words and scenes, no big deal. Then you say, “No more rated R, I’ll stick to PG-13.” Then you realize, “Actually, there’s enough filth in PG-13 too.” Then you start lowering your gaze when walking around campus or at the mall. Once you lower your gaze, you realize, “Maybe I’m too casual with sisters on campus. Maybe I should keep interactions strictly professional, like I would with my own sister.”

Your sensitivities start developing. You become more and more disturbed by things around you that didn’t used to bother you. People who were non-Muslim and lived a certain lifestyle, once they become Muslim and find themselves in an inappropriate gathering, their hearts become disturbed. They sense the filth affecting their heart just from being exposed to that environment. They’re constantly working to cleanse themselves—وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِلزَّكَوٰةِ فَـٰعِلُونَ.

Nobody can do this work for you. Speeches and talks can’t make you a better person. The only one who can work on this for yourself is you.

Ayah 5-7

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَـٰفِظُونَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ فَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَىٰ وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْعَادُونَ

“And those who guard their private parts, except with their spouses or what their right hands possess, for indeed they are not to be blamed. But whoever seeks beyond that, then those are the transgressors.”

The previous ayah mentioned cleansing oneself, and now Allah ﷻ explicitly highlights this particular aspect: وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَـٰفِظُونَ—“those who guard their privates.” They are constantly on guard regarding their shame and temptations.

Of all the sins humans can be tempted by—lying, backbiting, stealing, killing—Allah ﷻ consistently highlights shamelessness. Even when mentioning avoiding major sins in general, He specifically emphasizes الْفَوَاحِشَ (shameless acts). Why this special emphasis? Because this will be your first problem, and it’s a problem that won’t go away. This struggle began with the first human beings—when Adam and Hawwa (AS) realized their mistake, what did they immediately do? They tried to cover their shame with leaves from Paradise.

This is one of the hardest projects of our time. Shamelessness has always existed wherever human beings have had temptation and teenagers have had hormones. But we’re living in a particularly difficult era because shamelessness has become virtual, mobile, accessible at the tip of your finger. It was already hard when these khutbahs were being given to Muslims in the 1970s and 80s in the United States—living in a society more permissive than Muslim societies, where people dress and behave publicly in ways completely unacceptable to Islamic norms. We see it constantly; it becomes normalized for us.

But now the challenge has intensified exponentially. The pornography industry generates more revenue than Microsoft, Google, and the entire tech sector combined—trillions of dollars globally. It represents a massive portion of what is downloaded and uploaded on the web. We cannot underestimate its power and reach. When something is that huge of an economy, there’s tremendous force behind making you a consumer of that product, making our children consumers at every turn. There’s a suggestion, a trigger, everywhere we look.

We, more than any society before us, must engage in a really active battle against this. It has become as common as oxygen itself. فَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَىٰ وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكَ—“Whoever pursues beyond that.” The word ابْتَغَىٰ (ibtagha) implies a strong pursuit, like gold diggers during the California Gold Rush actively seeking and searching. Why would someone pursue beyond their lawful relationship? Because they don’t try to find beauty in their own marriage. They’re too addicted to filth on the internet, TV, or media. They compare what they see on screens to who they have at home and think, “This isn’t as interesting.” They pursue beyond that, preferring to get pleasure from a screen.

This is a massive problem we cannot underestimate. When the scum industry is this enormous, we have to recognize we’re in an active battle. فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْعَادُونَ—“those are the transgressors.” عَادُونَ comes from عُدْوَان (udwan)—aggression, crossing the line, becoming enemies of themselves and their families through this transgression.

The ayahs also mention إِلَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ—“except with their spouses or what their right hands possess.” This phrase مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ refers to a historical reality that requires understanding. Before Islam in Arabia, slavery existed, and slave owners could do whatever they wished with enslaved women without any consequences—they were considered property, no different than livestock.

Islam didn’t change social structures overnight, but it fundamentally transformed the relationship. Islam established that marriage is legitimate because there’s a contract—an agreement that transfers responsibility from the woman’s father or guardian to the husband. The marriage contract is actually a transfer of responsibilities: shelter, respect, food, clothing, education—everything the father used to provide, the husband now must provide.

When some Muslims already had female slaves before they became Muslim, Allah ﷻ said: “Now you owe them what you owe your wife.” Responsibilities were introduced where there weren’t any. The relationship that already existed was given structure and accountability. Conditions were revealed governing these interactions.

It’s crucial to note: this social reality was cleaned up and naturally died out. From the first generation of Companions (RA), when Umar (RA) encountered misinterpretation of these ayahs after Muslims conquered Roman lands—when some suggested Roman women should be considered “right hand possessions”—he firmly rejected this, saying, “That’s not what this means.”

The phrase مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ always appears in the past tense throughout the Quran—“what their right hands already possessed.” Never present tense, never future tense. This precision in the Quranic language is significant. The “right hand” itself is an expression of agreements and oaths—when you make an oath in Arabic, it’s literally called يَمِين (yamin), meaning right hand. This tells us that what was possessed came under an agreement, a contract, an understanding.

These ayahs also teach us that even the truest believers will not be able to fight temptation without marriage. You have to get married, and you have to have a healthy, good marriage. فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ—“they are not to be blamed” for fulfilling their natural needs within these lawful bounds.

Ayah 8

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَـٰنَـٰتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَٰعُونَ

“And those who, especially regarding what they’ve been entrusted with and their promises, are shepherds—watchful over them.”

Notice the beautiful progression: after addressing the personal issue of guarding oneself and maintaining loyalty to family, Allah ﷻ transitions to trustworthiness in broader society. This connection is profound.

There’s a business that runs on Christian principles where the CEO has a policy: if he discovers you cheated on your wife, you’re fired. His rationale? “If she can’t trust you and she lives with you, I can’t trust you in business.” It makes complete sense. Usually in the business world we say personal matters are separate, but he argues, “Your personal business is my business because I can’t have employees I can’t trust. She lives with you and can’t trust you—how can I?”

The previous ayah was about loyalty to your family and keeping your word with Allah ﷻ. That extends here: when you have that kind of loyalty in your family and that kind of faithfulness, you become a person who is trustworthy outside. We’re learning a very powerful social reality—you’re going to have a corrupt society if you have corruption in the family. You’ll have corrupt business, corrupt employees, corrupt employers, corrupt lending institutions. All of it stems from the corrupt seed of broken trust in the family.

If you don’t have shame left, you have no decency left. Then all these other indecencies—being dishonest, not keeping people’s trust—become easy for you. You’ve lost the most important aspect of your decency, so everything else is simple in comparison.

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَـٰنَـٰتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَٰعُونَ—“those who regarding their trusts and their promises are shepherds.” The word أَمَانَة comes from أَمْن —peace, safety. When someone entrusts you with something, they feel at peace, totally fine handing it to you. That’s amanah.

عَهْد is a promise where you take responsibility. The word رَاعُون—shepherds—comes from the hadith: “Every one of you is a shepherd, and every one of you will be asked about his flock.” They watch over their promises and trusts like a shepherd watches over sheep.

This extends beyond physical items someone asks you to keep. It includes work—when your employer trusts you with responsibilities. It includes school—when your parents pay your tuition, that’s a trust. They’re trusting you to take care of your education, do your homework, attend your classes. If the community sends you to an educational program, don’t cut class. Even if you’re bored, even if you don’t understand—you’re there on a trust. You have to sit and listen and learn because people trusted that you would come and benefit.

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَـٰنَـٰتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَٰعُونَ—when you say yes to something, finish it. If you can’t do it, just say no. An oath, a promise, is a heavy obligation hanging over your head. It’s mandated upon a decent person. If you can’t fulfill it, just say no from the beginning. People won’t call you a liar for being honest about your limitations—just say you can’t do it.

Ayah 9

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَوَٰتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ

“And those who, especially over their multiple prayers, are guardians—they maintain them.”

Notice how the surah began with صَلَاة in the singular—the institution of prayer—and now concludes with صَلَوَات in the plural. This progression is significant. The singular form referred to the obligatory prayers establishing khushu’. Now the plural form includes not just the essential prayers but additional prayers: the sunnah after Maghrib, after Isha, after Dhuhr, before Dhuhr, before Fajr, the witr prayer, tahajjud—all these prayers beyond the fard.

In our communities, we’ve often become people who pray only the fard and leave immediately. These true believers understand that salah itself is something you must guard, and you guard it with sunnah prayers. If you’re taking care of the additional prayers, the fard prayers are definitely protected. But if you’re not guarding the sunnah prayers, then your last line of defense is the fard itself. One slip-up and even the obligatory prayer could be compromised. So they go out of their way to create an extra defensive barrier around the fard prayers through the sunnah.

Also notice: everything else in these ayahs used the ism (noun form), indicating permanence and consistency. Here, Allah ﷻ uses the fi’l (verb) يُحَافِظُونَ—“they maintain, they guard”—because you can’t always perform all the sunnah prayers. Sometimes circumstances prevent it. So there’s a relaxation here; it’s okay. But at least they continually try. They’re consistent in their effort to guard these multiple prayers, even if they can’t always achieve it perfectly.

Ayah 10-11

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْوَٰرِثُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسَ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

“Those—they are the inheritors. Those who will inherit Al-Firdaws, in which they will remain forever.”

After describing all these qualities, Allah ﷻ concludes: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْوَٰرِثُونَ—“Those are the inheritors.” When the ayah ends like that without immediately specifying what they inherit, it creates anticipation, a sense of mystery. What are they inheriting?

The next ayah answers: ٱلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسَ—“Those who will inherit Al-Firdaws.” الْفِرْدَوْس is originally a Persian word meaning a lush green valley, a garden filled with all types of fruits and flowers. In Islamic understanding, it refers to the highest levels of Paradise.

هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ—“They will remain in it forever.” They will constantly, eternally remain in that beautiful valley of Paradise.

May Allah ﷻ make us from among them.

The Checklist of True Believers:

  1. They have khushu’ in their salah
  2. They turn away from vain talk and useless activities
  3. They constantly work on purifying and improving their character
  4. They guard their modesty and shame (mentioned in three ayahs, showing its critical importance)
  5. They are trustworthy with what they’re entrusted and faithful to their promises
  6. They guard their prayers, both obligatory and additional

Notice that out of these qualities, one is about salah at the beginning, one about salah at the end, one about avoiding vain conversation, one about self-improvement, three about guarding one’s shame, and one about trusts and promises. The proportional emphasis reveals what matters most to becoming a true believer whose ultimate success is already guaranteed.

Ayah 12–53 · linked video: 02. Al-Mu’minun (Ayah 12-53)

Surah Al-Muminun: Commentary on Ayahs 12-53

Ayah 12

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن سُلَـٰلَةٍ مِّن طِينٍ

We already created the human being from سُلَـٰلَةٍ, an extract from طِينٍ (clay or dirt). The word sulalah comes from the verb salla, which means to pull a sword out of its sheath, to extract the essence of something. When you squeeze soil to get the most useful nutrients out of it, that’s also from this root. So sulalah refers to the most nutrient-rich, the juiciest, the most useful part of the soil - the extract from teen that the human being was made from.

Ayah 13

ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ نُطْفَةً فِى قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ

Then We turned him into a نُطْفَةً (droplet) فِى قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ (in a place, in a resting place that is very stable and strong). Allah ﷻ describes the uterus of the mother as a very strong organ that can handle tremendous stress. We know that childbirth is an incredibly traumatic experience, yet the mother’s body can withstand that entire ordeal and still recover, then bear a child again and again. It’s a qaraar makeen, a very strong, stable place for the baby.

This is especially remarkable in those early phases when impregnation happens. The mother continues her day without knowing, sitting up and down, walking around, and the embryo stays secure where it rests. That’s the qaraar makeen, the station where it remains at rest.

Ayah 14

ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا ٱلنُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا ٱلْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا ٱلْمُضْغَةَ عِظَـٰمًا فَكَسَوْنَا ٱلْعِظَـٰمَ لَحْمًا ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَـٰهُ خَلْقًا ءَاخَرَ ۚ فَتَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقِينَ

Then We created that nutfah into an عَلَقَةً (hanging piece of flesh). The word comes from the verb “to hang.” Classical scholars, limited by their time, interpreted this as clots of blood based on what they observed in miscarriages. But alaqah itself has nothing to do with blood - it refers to something that hangs. Modern embryology confirms that the piece of flesh hangs from the uterus, which then develops into the baby.

Then We morphed the alaqah into مُضْغَةً (something that looks like chewed flesh). Imagine taking chewing gum and biting into it, leaving teeth marks. That’s what the early embryo looks like inside the womb - a chewed piece of flesh.

Then We took that mudghah and started forming عِظَـٰمًا (bones). The early form is cartilage, which eventually becomes bone. فَكَسَوْنَا ٱلْعِظَـٰمَ لَحْمًا (Then We dressed the bones with flesh). Kasawna means to clothe, to dress. Just as we dress orphans when they come seeking help, Allah ﷻ dresses the bones with flesh in the womb. This same expression appears again in reference to the Day of Resurrection - we call upon Allah ﷻ, Al-Kashiy al-Idhaam lahman ba’dal mawt, the One who will dress the bones with flesh again after death has come.

Notice the progression: نُطْفَةً… ثُمَّ, then three successive stages with فَـ : فَخَلَقْنَا… فَخَلَقْنَا… فَكَسَوْنَا. The first thumma implies a significant break - from being just dirt to having the ability to become a droplet. Then three rapid developments at 40, 40, 40 days each, as described by the Prophet ﷺ. After the third period of 40 days - meaning 120 days total - what happens?

ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَـٰهُ خَلْقًا ءَاخَرَ (Then We elevated him and created him into khalqan aakhar - another creation entirely). Notice the return to thumma, indicating another major transition. The word ansha’nahu means both to create and to raise or elevate. This is beautifully appropriate because the human being is about to be raised in status.

This embryonic development happens in animals - cows, monkeys, birds. But with human beings, at a certain stage, the child transforms into something completely different because the ruh (soul) is blown into the child. This is the khalq aakhar, the entirely different creation.

فَتَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقِينَ (How full of blessings Allah is! The best of all creators!) The word tabaraka is mentioned here because there’s a special barakah from Allah ﷻ. He uses ahsan al-khaliqeen (the best of creators) because the human being is the best of His creation, as He Himself says: “We created the human being in the best of forms” (95:4).

The word barakah also implies that more good comes out than expected. Because the ruh has been blown into this child, this creature will produce more good than any other creature. That’s the expectation built into human creation.

Ayah 15

ثُمَّ إِنَّكُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ

After all of that remarkable creation, you are absolutely going to die. No escaping it.

Ayah 16

ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ تُبْعَثُونَ

Then, without any doubt, when the Day of Resurrection arrives, you will be raised again.

Ayah 17

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرَآئِقَ وَمَا كُنَّا عَنِ ٱلْخَلْقِ غَـٰفِلِينَ

We have created above you سَبْعَ طَرَآئِقَ (seven pathways). Multiple interpretations exist for this phrase. The dominant scholarly position is that we don’t know precisely what it means. Some say it refers to the seven heavens. Others suggest it refers to the journey of the soul after death - the wretched soul is turned back after the first heaven, but the righteous soul is taken up through all seven levels, one road after another, altogether seven pathways.

وَمَا كُنَّا عَنِ ٱلْخَلْقِ غَـٰفِلِينَ (And We have never been unaware of what We’ve created). This entire expansive universe may be unknown to you, but We have never been someone who didn’t know about what We created. Allah ﷻ keeps watch over all of His creation.

Ayah 18

وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءًۢ بِقَدَرٍ فَأَسْكَنَّـٰهُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰ ذَهَابٍۭ بِهِۦ لَقَـٰدِرُونَ

We sent water down from the sky بِقَدَرٍ (with perfect calculation, with exact measure). Which land will get how much rain this year? Where will every single drop fall? There’s a beautiful continuity of imagery here: just as the little droplet impregnates the woman, the water that drops from the sky impregnates the earth, and life comes forth.

فَأَسْكَنَّـٰهُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (Then We make it settle into the land). We make the water give life to the rivers, feed the oceans, nourish the earth. وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰ ذَهَابٍۭ بِهِۦ لَقَـٰدِرُونَ (And We are completely capable of removing it). Don’t think I cannot make droughts happen or rivers dry up entirely. The water is completely under My control.

Ayah 19

فَأَنشَأْنَا لَكُم بِهِۦ جَنَّـٰتٍ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَـٰبٍ لَّكُمْ فِيهَا فَوَٰكِهُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ

By means of this water, We raised for you gardens of palm trees and grapes. لَّكُمْ فِيهَا فَوَٰكِهُ كَثِيرَةٌ (In which there are for you many kinds of fruits). The word فَوَٰكِهُ refers to fruits that taste so good that when you bite into them, they make you smile. It comes from fakaha in Arabic, which means to smile, to be happy, to be joyous. The fruit tastes so good you can’t help but smile.

وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (And of them, ones that even you people eat). There are so many more varieties of fruit than what you Arabs in your environment and climate get to experience. Your region only allows for certain kinds, but there are many more. Yet some of them, even you get to eat.

Ayah 20

وَشَجَرَةً تَخْرُجُ مِن طُورِ سَيْنَآءَ تَنۢبُتُ بِٱلدُّهْنِ وَصِبْغٍ لِّلْـَٔاكِلِينَ

We also created a tree that stems from طُورِ سَيْنَآءَ (Mount Sinai), تَنۢبُتُ بِٱلدُّهْنِ (one that sprouts and as it rises, produces oil along with it) - olive oil. Allah ﷻ highlights the power and blessed nature of this tree in this Surah, and in the next Surah (An-Nur), He will give us an entire parable based on its oil in the passage about the Light of Allah ﷻ.

وَصِبْغٍ لِّلْـَٔاكِلِينَ - this is one of my favorite words in the Quran. Sibgh actually comes from sabagha, and sibgh means color. But sabagha also means cloth that has been colored or dyed. It’s used for clothes stained with stew. Sibgh is also used for bread, which is like cloth, and you dip it into the stew and it gets colored.

Allah ﷻ creates this entire image: someone preparing a meal, making a stew, using the olive oil, then taking bread and dipping it so the bread gets colored. Before they take that bite into their mouth, that’s sibgh lil-akileen - a colored dip for those who are going to eat. These kinds of images in the Quran highlight how closely Allah ﷻ watches us. It’s as if He’s saying: I love watching when you dip the bread in that stew.

Ayah 21

وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ لَعِبْرَةً ۖ نُّسْقِيكُم مِّمَّا فِى بُطُونِهَا وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ

When it comes to cattle - cows, sheep and similar animals - there is definitely a real lesson and warning for you. نُّسْقِيكُم مِّمَّا فِى بُطُونِهَا (We give you a drink from whatever is in its belly). We learned about this before: the milk comes from between filth and blood. If you cut the animal open, you don’t get milk. But when Allah ﷻ extracts it, it comes out pure and you drink from it.

وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ كَثِيرَةٌ (And for you in those cattle there are many benefits). وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (And among them are some that you get to eat).

Ayah 22

وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ

Upon the camels, cows and larger animals you get to ride - you are loaded onto them. And onto ships you are boarded, you are carried. They become means of transportation. And after you travel with a camel and it gets too old or tired, at least now you have dinner. Then you take its skin and turn it into a tent. It’s a complete survival kit.

Ayah 23

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

The mention of الْفُلْكِ (the ship, the large vessel) is remarkable here. What messenger is immediately reminded when ships are mentioned? Nuh (AS). So the narrative flows directly: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا (We sent Nuh) to his nation. فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (He said: My nation, enslave yourselves to Allah). مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ (You have no one worthy of worship except Him, no one at all). أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (Then why aren’t you conscious? Why don’t you protect yourselves?)

Notice that the passage we just read was all about appreciating Allah ﷻ - the creation stages, the water, the fruits, the cattle. It’s a continuation of the previous Surah’s theme: “They did not appreciate Allah as He deserves to be appreciated.” So Allah ﷻ highlighted things we should appreciate about Him. But then He chose messengers from among His angels and human beings to deliver this message to humanity. The transition is remarkably smooth - from ship to Nuh (AS).

Ayah 24

فَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُرِيدُ أَن يَتَفَضَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَنزَلَ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً مَّا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِىٓ ءَابَآئِنَا ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

The الْمَلَؤُا۟ (chieftains, the council) - the word mala as a verb (mala’a) means to fill. The mala are called the chiefs or generals because they fill the court of the king. They’re the ones sitting right by him, filling his room. In old tribal systems, these were the elders who sat together in council.

When they held these tribunal hearings, the entire town would gather to hear what they had to say. It was like the news broadcast of ancient times, the media of old days - a special news alert. What did they declare?

مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ (This is nothing but a human being just like you people. Don’t be too impressed with him. He’s just like you, nothing special.) يُرِيدُ أَن يَتَفَضَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ (However, there is one difference: he wants to impose his superiority on you. He wants to make sure you think of him as more preferred, more blessed than you. He’s self-righteous.)

The elders are talking, trying to convince their people: وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَنزَلَ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً (Had Allah really wanted, He would have sent angels). مَّا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِىٓ ءَابَآئِنَا ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (We didn’t hear of any messengers coming in our ancestry. What is this messenger business? We don’t understand.)

Ayah 25

إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌۢ بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌ فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ بِهِۦ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ

Then they came up with another answer: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌۢ بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌ (He’s nothing but a man possessed by a jinn, possessed by a demon. That’s his problem.) فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ بِهِۦ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (Just wait around for him. He’ll go away. He’ll die off. Don’t make any rash decisions. Don’t become Muslims. Just give him some time - he’s going to go away.)

They made this public declaration. The entire council of chiefs called the whole village together and announced: “This is our public policy. This is our attitude toward Nuh.”

Ayah 26

قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ

Nuh (AS) responds: رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ (Master, aid me against the lies they make against me). This is a beautiful set of ayahs for understanding what our dua is supposed to be against media attacks on Islam. The chiefs were the ancient media. They made their attacks and declared their policy: “He’s crazy, he’s possessed, he’s an extremist.” The only thing the messenger could do was make sincere dua and continue to carry his message.

Ayah 27

فَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ أَنِ ٱصْنَعِ ٱلْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا وَفَارَ ٱلتَّنُّورُ ۙ فَٱسْلُكْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَوْلُ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تُخَـٰطِبْنِى فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ

فَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ أَنِ ٱصْنَعِ ٱلْفُلْكَ (Then We revealed to him: Manufacture the ship). بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا (Under Our watch and Our inspiration). Not only will you build this ship under My watch, I will inspire you where to put each plank, where to put each nail, at what angle to place this, at what angle to place that. The entire architectural blueprint will be revealed to you, placed in your heart and mind, and you’ll work accordingly.

Building something without a plan is impossible. You put two planks together and realize they don’t fit right - you have to fix this, fix that. But Allah ﷻ says you’ll do it under Our watch and instruction. Just like a construction project needs a manager who tells workers what to do, and behind the manager there must be an architect with full blueprints specifying materials, stress loads, everything. Without meticulous construction, it might look good but the moment it’s tested, it will fall apart.

Nuh (AS) required stable construction. Why? This boat wasn’t going in a swimming pool. It would face one of the greatest floods in human history and had to survive. On top of that, it eventually had to land on a mountain and still survive, and when it landed it needed to be level so it wouldn’t tip over and everyone would fall. Allah ﷻ revealed the entire construction plan.

فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا وَفَارَ ٱلتَّنُّورُ (Then when Our final decision came and the pot finally boiled over). This is a beautiful phrase in Arabic - fara at-tannur. It’s used for when you lose your temper: you’ve been holding it in, holding it in, but now the kettle has boiled over. For 950 years Nuh (AS) called his people, and they kept angering Allah ﷻ. It’s like water being slowly boiled on a stove - the bubbles are small at first, then they get bigger and bigger, steam comes out, and if you don’t let the steam out, eventually it blows over and spills. Allah’s anger spilled out. They pulled the last straw.

But literally, it’s also beautiful because the entire valley they lived in is compared to a pot, and the flooding and rains and waters from the oceans rose so much that it was as if the pot tipped over and poured into the valley, just like water boils out of a pot and spills everywhere.

فَٱسْلُكْ فِيهَا (Then insert into it). This word tells us it wasn’t a primitive boat like children draw - a flat thing with animals on top. If that were the case, Allah would say “put them on it” (alayha). But اسْلُكْ فِيهَا means stick them inside, insert them all the way in - like when Musa (AS) was told “usluk yadaka fee jaybika” (stick your hand inside your armpit and it will come out white).

He made an entire cruise liner with quarters, entire barns for the animals inside. مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ (From every pair, make sure you have a male and female of every species you can find and put them inside). He made this elaborate ship to take the animals into.

وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَوْلُ مِنْهُمْ (And your family, except whoever upon them My word has already come). Meaning: I’ve told you who is supposed to be saved, who is invited onto the ship, who I’ve issued tickets to. Everybody else is meant to be dead - past tense. They haven’t even died yet, but Allah ﷻ says don’t worry about it, they are as good as dead.

وَلَا تُخَـٰطِبْنِى فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ (And don’t even talk to Me about those who’ve done wrong). Don’t even bring it up. إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ (They are ones who will be drowned). There’s a difference in Arabic between ghariq and mughraq. Ghariq is someone drowning, and there’s an expression “more hopeless than the drowning one.” But mughraq means the drowned one - someone whose head is grabbed and stuck under the water until they drown to death. Allah ﷻ describes that even if they knew how to swim and tried to climb, a divine force would push them into the water. They’re not going to drown on their own - they’re going to be drowned.

Ayah 28

فَإِذَا ٱسْتَوَيْتَ أَنتَ وَمَن مَّعَكَ عَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ فَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى نَجَّىٰنَا مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

فَإِذَا ٱسْتَوَيْتَ (Then when you become balanced, when you become even). In other words, the ship is going to land and become level. While the flood was happening, what was happening to the ship? It was going this way and that way, back and forth. Nobody on the ship could even stand up - that’s what we’re learning. But when the waters calm down and you notice the ship has settled and anchored (the anchor being Mount Judi), أَنتَ وَمَن مَّعَكَ عَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ (you and everybody with you on the ship), فَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ (then say Alhamdulillah).

“Don’t ask Me about those who got drowned. The fact that you got rescued - you say: All praise and gratitude belongs to Allah.” ٱلَّذِى نَجَّىٰنَا مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (The One who rescued us from the evil nation, from the wrongdoing nation).

Ayah 29

وَقُل رَّبِّ أَنزِلْنِى مُنزَلًا مُّبَارَكًا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْمُنزِلِينَ

وَقُل رَّبِّ أَنزِلْنِى مُنزَلًا مُّبَارَكًا (And say: Master, bring me down a blessed descent, give me a landing that’s a blessed landing). A landing full of goodness. This time we’re coming down - it’s peculiar that Allah ﷻ sent Adam (AS) down from Jannah to the earth, and now He’s sending Nuh (AS) down in a second descent. No human being was left except those on the ship, so he’s being sent down again on the earth from the ship. This is also a kind of descent.

He says: This time around, send me down a blessed kind of descent. مُنزَلًا مُّبَارَكًا - munzal is a descent but it’s also a place and time of descent. Send the ship down at the right time and the right place. The place itself should be blessed. وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْمُنزِلِينَ (And You are the best of those who can send down, the One who can make it land properly).

You can understand this dua: the water is receding and they realize there are mountains all around them. If the water is receding and there’s an obstacle ahead, the ship will crash and everyone dies. In a mountain range, for the ship to land on top of a mountain and be level - that’s asking Allah ﷻ to find a safe place to land. Even if the land is slightly unstable, they’re dead. Find me the right place to land, because if it’s not stable, we won’t survive.

There are very few words here, but they capture so much scenery.

Ayah 30

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ وَإِن كُنَّا لَمُبْتَلِينَ

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ (In that particularly there are many miraculous signs). وَإِن كُنَّا لَمُبْتَلِينَ (And We have always been ones that put people through tough tests. We truly do give very serious tests). It’s as if Nuh’s (AS) tests didn’t even end with the nation being drowned. Another test was the ship landing. That’s a big deal too.

Ayah 31

ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا ءَاخَرِينَ

ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا ءَاخَرِينَ (Then We raised, much after them, another generation).

Ayah 32

فَأَرْسَلْنَا فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

فَأَرْسَلْنَا فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ (Then We sent a messenger among them). Allah ﷻ doesn’t even mention the name of the messenger this time, as if to say: This story happened over and over and over again. After Nuh (AS), We sent a messenger. People might wonder, “Which messenger?” Any messenger. Every messenger.

أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ (That you should enslave yourselves to Allah. You have no one to worship except Allah, no one to obey, no one to consider an authority). أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (Why don’t you people protect yourselves? Why don’t you take precaution?)

The exact same language as Nuh (AS). This is Allah’s way of saying: Look at the tragedy of history. It repeats itself. The human being puts himself in the same loss over and over and over again.

Ayah 33

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَتْرَفْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يَأْكُلُ مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ مِنْهُ وَيَشْرَبُ مِمَّا تَشْرَبُونَ

Some new chiefs, some new tribunal comes along: وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ (The leaders of his nation, those who disbelieved, said). وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ (They denied and lied against the meeting of the afterlife). This interesting phrasing means they will meet the afterlife, they will meet the Day of Judgment.

Now Allah ﷻ adds details to help us understand this archetype - this template that repeats. There’s a certain kind of person, a certain kind of group that comes in every nation and generation and civilization, making the same mistakes. Allah ﷻ gives them the same opportunities, messengers say the same things. This unnamed rasul is an archetype, a sample of all messengers. This nation and its elite are an archetype of all elite in all nations who have a particular attitude.

What does Allah ﷻ say about them? They’re elite, they disbelieve, they deny the afterlife - it’s a joke to them. And another crucial point: وَأَتْرَفْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا (We had given them luxury and amenities and a good lifestyle in this worldly life). They have an elite, luxurious lifestyle. The political elite aren’t necessarily supposed to be the financial elite - that’s actually the case in corrupt societies. In corrupt societies, the political elite and financial elite are one and the same.

This subject came before in Surat Al-Isra when we talked about when Allah ﷻ decides to destroy a nation: “We give command and power to the financially elite” - political power to the financial elite. This is visible in elections nowadays, the rhetoric about who can or can’t relate to the average person. When you’re extremely wealthy, you haven’t seen money as small as a $20 bill. You don’t understand what that means - it’s beyond your comprehension. You never see cash, you have platinum credit cards, everything’s automated, your servants do the shopping. You don’t know what it means to stand in line with food stamps.

The financial elite become disconnected from society. As a result, they can only relate to and take care of the needs of other financial elite. When that happens, who suffers? The masses suffer, the poor suffer. A societal divide is created that eventually erupts and leads to chaos.

The Hindu kingdoms before Islam came to India were financial and political elite simultaneously. Corrupt governments across the world will be financial and political elite. You’ll have popular uprisings where they pick a leader from the people, but once he gains political power and controls the power plants, gas lines, international trade, a slice starts coming his way - the slice supposed to go to the people. He becomes political elite and ends up becoming financial elite, and the same corruption starts all over again.

The principles of how an Islamic government works can change in their mechanics, but the principles don’t change. Umar (RA) would hire people in public office - mayor, governor, we can think of it as senator nowadays - and he would do an audit. What’s the audit? The moment the person got hired: What are your assets right now? How many outfits do you own? How many clothes? How many shoes? A full audit. Then the person serves ten years and is leaving office, and Umar (RA) says: Let’s do an audit again. “You used to own eight pairs of shoes when you came into office. Now you own ten. Those two pairs belong to the people - give them back.” You should leave office with the same thing you came with, because you’re in public service, which means you can’t be in self-service. You can’t go into public office looking for contracts on the side, then become a consultant afterwards, become a lobbyist making millions on top.

If you’re going to be a public servant, you’re going to be a public servant. You can’t build your own assets. It’s a sacrifice, a burden.

But here, أَتْرَفْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - We hooked them up in worldly life. مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ (This is nothing but flesh and skin like you, a human being, nothing special). يَأْكُلُ مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ مِنْهُ (He even eats what you eat. What makes him so spiritual? I eat the same food and I don’t get spiritual inclination from God). وَيَشْرَبُ مِمَّا تَشْرَبُونَ (And he drinks what you drink. What makes him special?)

Ayah 34

وَلَئِنْ أَطَعْتُم بَشَرًا مِّثْلَكُمْ إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ

وَلَئِنْ أَطَعْتُم بَشَرًا مِّثْلَكُمْ (And if you end up following a fool just like yourselves), إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ (then you’re going to be losers).

The financial elite look at the rest of society as beneath them. They tell them: “You people are uneducated. You don’t have the sophistication to make the right decisions for your country. If you pick one of your own, you won’t be able to. You don’t understand big decisions - we have experience in those things. If you follow one of your own, you’re making a huge mistake.”

Recently in an election campaign, there was a fundraiser for one of the parties. Multimillionaires came to the fundraiser, aristocrats. Each plate was $100,000 or whatever. They were being interviewed outside, and this woman said: “The working class just doesn’t understand politics. They’re going to vote the wrong way because they just don’t understand how things work.”

When the elite speak, they speak about the people as immature: “You don’t know what to do. Listen to us. We got this rich and powerful because obviously we’re smarter, so you better listen to us.” To this day, the same attitudes exist.

Ayah 35

أَيَعِدُكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ إِذَا مِتُّمْ وَكُنتُمْ تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَنَّكُم مُّخْرَجُونَ

أَيَعِدُكُمْ (Is he promising you?) أَنَّكُمْ إِذَا مِتُّمْ وَكُنتُمْ تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا (That you people, when you die and you’ve been reduced to dirt and bones), أَنَّكُم مُّخْرَجُونَ (that you’re going to be brought back again, extracted again? Is that what he’s promising? You believe that? How gullible are you people?)

Religion is the opiate of the poor masses, they think. You people don’t have higher education - that’s why these things appeal to you. That’s why churches are filled in poor neighborhoods and masajid too. Religion offers a better life in the next life because they have a horrible life here, so at least they can hope for better in the afterlife. This philosophy has actually translated into modern policies.

The Sanitation Department cleans garbage, but in modern and postmodern society, that’s what church and masjid chaplaincy has become to them. Have a chaplain come to the prison, to the hospital for people who don’t have hope, who are depressed. At least give them that.

There was even a Times Magazine cover in the nineties about religion in the workplace - a Muslim making salah. Muslims were so happy: “Wow, they’re talking about Muslims in the workplace!” But actually, what they were saying was: when these people are religious, they become better workers, so they’re better for our bottom line.

They did a study of Taco Bell employees in Northern California, some of the most expensive real estate in the country. These employees couldn’t afford housing on their wages. They had makeshift communities with tin roof homes, the entire block sharing one electricity line. These people were seriously depressed. Taco Bell hired chaplains to give sermons about hope, about life being better in paradise - not because they cared about their spiritual wellbeing, but because they said: “If these people get too depressed, we’ll lose our employees. We need to keep them. Give them at least some hope so they’ll stay.”

This is what religion is to them. Let them have their hope in the afterlife. They look down on it. You reach that kind of elitism where belief in the afterlife is a form of weakness.

I was interviewed by Dollars and Cents magazine when I was in college. The guy asked me: “Is this a phase you’re going through? You’ve got problems in life, that’s why you talk so much about the afterlife and paradise and hellfire - because you can’t deal with your problems right now. Is that what it is?”

These archetypes that Allah ﷻ describes in the Quran are alive and well to this day.

Ayah 36

۞ هَيْهَاتَ هَيْهَاتَ لِمَا تُوعَدُونَ

هَيْهَاتَ هَيْهَاتَ (Come on! Seriously! No way!) لِمَا تُوعَدُونَ (There’s no way this whole afterlife thing is going to happen).

Hayhaat is hard to translate. It means far-fetched. هَيْهَاتَ هَيْهَاتَ - “No way, man! This isn’t happening!” Hayhaat is also used when you scold somebody, when someone hopes for something and you get in their face and say “No, it ain’t gonna happen!” It’s done to inform someone in the strongest way that what they’re hoping for will never happen. Someone says “I really hope they win” and you respond “They’re never gonna win! Oh my God, that’s so crazy! I can’t believe you’re so dumb to believe that!” That’s hayhaat.

“What craziness do you people believe in? It’s never gonna happen, what you’ve been promised!”

Ayah 37

إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ

إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا (You people have to understand - this worldly life is all we have). نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا (We’re living right now, we’ll die, and that’s it. This is YOLO - you only live once). وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ (And nobody’s getting raised. Nobody’s getting raised at all.)

Ayah 38

إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا وَمَا نَحْنُ لَهُۥ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ

إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا (I’m telling you, he’s just another guy. He’s nothing special. He just made up lies about or against God).

Now they’re rallying the people. First they were talking down to them, now they bring them together: وَمَا نَحْنُ لَهُۥ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (And WE are never going to believe in him. We’re not going to cave in to him, especially to him.)

Ayah 39

قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ

قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ (So the messenger - whoever this messenger is - says: My Master, aid me against the lies they make against me.)

Ayah 40

قَالَ عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ لَّيُصْبِحُنَّ نَـٰدِمِينَ

قَالَ عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ (Allah ﷻ said: After just a little bit more time), لَّيُصْبِحُنَّ نَـٰدِمِينَ (they are going to be so ashamed and full of regret over what they were saying, over how they lived).

Ayah 41

فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ بِٱلْحَقِّ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ غُثَآءً ۚ فَبُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ بِٱلْحَقِّ (Then the loud explosion, the scream, took them by shock, and it took them truthfully, for a real purpose).

When a natural disaster happens, people say “Why would such a thing happen? There’s no rhyme or reason for something like that.” Allah ﷻ describes that when the nation that rejects messengers is finally destroyed, when that natural disaster comes, that loud explosion comes بِٱلْحَقِّ - it comes with purpose. It truly does come for a reason.

فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ غُثَآءً (Then We turned them into ghuthaa’ - the foam that floodwaters wash away). When destruction comes and floods follow, there’s foam at the end of the wave that carries pebbles, straws, junk on the ground. Allah ﷻ says We turned them into the junk that floodwaters wash away. Their corpses just wash up as the waves come.

فَبُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (Then away with the wrongdoing nation! Let them get lost!) Bu’dan is hard to translate - it’s like saying “Get lost! Get out of here! Get out of My face!” In old English: “Away with you!” It’s condemnation - away with them, I’m done with these people. Allah ﷻ talks about them with disgust.

Ayah 42

ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قُرُونًا ءَاخَرِينَ

ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قُرُونًا ءَاخَرِينَ (Then after them, We raised many other generations). It kept happening. It didn’t happen once - it kept coming and kept coming.

Ayah 43

مَا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ

مَا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا (There’s not a single nation that will ever get ahead of its deadline). No nation will live an extra day beyond when it’s supposed to die. وَمَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ (And they’re not going to delay it). They won’t die sooner and they won’t procrastinate dying. They won’t die after they’re supposed to.

Just like a person’s death is absolutely inevitable and written, nations also live and die. Nations have a lifespan and cannot get ahead of it, cannot die sooner, won’t die after they’re supposed to. Just as a person’s death is written, a nation’s death is written.

Ayah 44

ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا تَتْرَا ۖ كُلَّ مَا جَآءَ أُمَّةً رَّسُولُهَا كَذَّبُوهُ ۚ فَأَتْبَعْنَا بَعْضَهُم بَعْضًا وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ ۚ فَبُعْدًا لِّقَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ

ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا تَتْرَا (Then We kept sending Our messengers, one after another, after another, after another).

Now we’re learning something amazing about the sending of messengers. Because of the word tatra - messengers were sent all over the planet to all generations of people, all nations, all ethnicities, all backgrounds. But when were messengers sent? Was the planet ever free from messengers? Was there ever a time when there were no messengers at all?

Allah ﷻ says تَتْرَا - they came one after another in succession without there ever being a real break. Either there’s a nabi or there’s a rasul, and there’s always been a rasul somewhere on the planet. There has always been a messenger until finally the final messenger.

Some say the break was never too long - there was never too long of a break. The longest break we know of in recorded history is between Isa (AS) and Muhammad ﷺ, which Allah ﷻ calls a fatrah - after a gap between the messengers. That 600-year gap of darkness is described in the Quran as the rise of a morning after a long night. That’s the imagery Allah ﷻ gives to it.

كُلَّ مَا جَآءَ أُمَّةً رَّسُولُهَا كَذَّبُوهُ (Every single time a nation’s messenger came to them, they lied against him). Notice it says كَذَّبُوهُ - they lied against him. The singular implies that messengers are men.

فَأَتْبَعْنَا بَعْضَهُم بَعْضًا (Then We continually followed one up after the other). وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ (And We turned them into news events, historical incidents that would be cited later on). فَبُعْدًا لِّقَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (Then away with the people who refuse to believe - they can just get lost).

This formula hasn’t changed ever. If it hasn’t changed ever, why do you think you’re the exception? Why do you think you’re just going to listen to this man and his message and nothing is going to come to you? I’ve done away with many nations before you.

Ayah 45

ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَأَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ

ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَأَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ (Then We sent Musa and his brother Harun), بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (with Our miraculous signs and an authoritative, clear proof).

Ayah 46

إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا عَالِينَ

إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ (To Fir’awn and his chieftains - again, chiefs, chiefs, chiefs). فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ (And they showed arrogance). وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا عَالِينَ (And they were a very high nation, a very arrogant nation).

Aali actually has a fascinating meaning. Aala by itself means somebody who talks about you with attitude, and you’re expected to lower your eyes and be scared of making eye contact. Anyone tries to act up, you just give them a look and they get back in their place. That guy is aali. These were elite people, arrogant people, intimidating people.

Ayah 47

فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَنُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ مِثْلِنَا وَقَوْمُهُمَا لَنَا عَـٰبِدُونَ

فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَنُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ مِثْلِنَا (We’re going to believe in two human beings that are just like us? Actually, they’re not even just like us.) وَقَوْمُهُمَا لَنَا عَـٰبِدُونَ (And their nation serves us, are slaves to us).

Aabidun doesn’t mean literal worship here - it means they actively enslave themselves to us, we put them to service. “Our slaves are going to talk back to us?”

A new rationale for elitism, a new rationale for rejecting the message: “If we’re going to believe that guy, he’s from an inferior race. He’s from a third world country, a lower economy. Why would we believe these people? They’re not elite like we are. How can they have anything better?”

Orientalist studies in the West - the study of Islam - for the most part approaches it from a Discovery Channel perspective. You know how on Discovery Channel they study animals? “How interesting - the lion hides behind the bush waiting for prey.” That’s the attitude with which they study Islam in many institutions. “How interesting - Muslim civilization. Peculiar practices in the month of Ramadan. Quite fantastic, really.” They’re studying it like this cute, inferior culture - like studying Aborigines or the koala bear in Australia. That’s how the studies are conducted - as if Muslims are beneath them.

They’re not going to teach us something. They study about them because it’s cute and interesting to know about other lesser civilizations. It’s fun to learn about those kinds of things, but obviously we are - as Francis Fukuyama says - the greatest of all civilizations ever. The famous contemporary philosopher argues that society will never get more evolved than we are.

In the colonial era it was more brash - it was called “the white man’s burden.” We have to bring civilization to everybody else. That’s just different, more politically correct terminology now, but the ideas are essentially the same. The more advanced world has to give the rest of the world an education in being real human beings.

“Why would they have anything to teach us? We are so far beyond them. Their nations serve us. We build our factories in their countries for cheap labor because our population is too evolved to work in those conditions. We can’t have those here, so we put them over there.”

Ayah 48

فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا فَكَانُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْلَكِينَ

فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا (So they lied against both Musa and Harun), فَكَانُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْلَكِينَ (and they were from those given violent death).

Muhlakin - those who die in a violent way. Someone falls off a cliff: halaka. Someone accidentally dies in a car accident: halaka. Someone is pushed off a cliff, literally pushed: halaka. They were given violent destruction. And what destroyed them? They were made to drown.

Notice the commonalities: Two messengers have been mentioned by name so far - which two? Nuh (AS), and then the pair of Musa and Harun (AS). What’s the common thread? The disbelieving nations were drowned. They were made to drown. The commonalities keep flowing, and it all started with the ship. The entire conversation started with the ship and flows into these subjects.

Ayah 49

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ (We gave Musa the Book, the Torah), لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (so that the people may be guided).

Ayah 50

وَجَعَلْنَا ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُۥٓ ءَايَةً وَءَاوَيْنَـٰهُمَآ إِلَىٰ رَبْوَةٍ ذَاتِ قَرَارٍ وَمَعِينٍ

وَجَعَلْنَا ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُۥٓ ءَايَةً (And We made the son of Maryam and his mother a miraculous sign). وَءَاوَيْنَـٰهُمَآ إِلَىٰ رَبْوَةٍ (And We gave them refuge to a high plateau of land).

This is interesting because Allah ﷻ uses the word awaynaa - to give refuge against danger. Some interpreters say this refers to when Maryam (AS) left the city, in danger of being ridiculed, mocked, insulted or even punished for being pregnant. She left to the outskirts to a high place. There was a maqarr (a safe place) and ma’een (water available). “Eat and drink,” the Quran says. That’s the place on the outskirts of Jerusalem where she went and had the child. Both of them were protected there.

Others say this refers to something else. The puppet king at the time in Israel was a Jewish king under Roman rule - a colonized king, similar to how in Indian colonization they had rajas and nawabs, titles given to locals (Muslims and Hindus) who were puppet rulers while the colonizers were the actual rulers, maintaining control through local figureheads.

According to biblical narrative that some Muslim historians have referenced, this Jewish king saw a dream that a child would be born who would wreck the kingdom. He ordered children to be executed. In this situation of fear, Maryam (AS) took Isa (AS) away from that community. The child was already known to be special because he had spoken in public. She took him to Egypt until he was about 10 or 12 years old, then brought him back.

So the high place could be Egypt - ذَاتِ قَرَارٍ وَمَعِينٍ (a place where she could remain safe with a wellspring of water). It could be either interpretation: those 10-12 years in Egypt that Christian sources tell us about, or the place where she gave birth that the Quran mentioned - a slightly elevated patch of land with more than average cultivation and a water spring.

Ayah 51

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرُّسُلُ كُلُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَٱعْمَلُوا۟ صَـٰلِحًا ۖ إِنِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرُّسُلُ (O messengers!) كُلُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ (Eat and consume from the good and pure things). وَٱعْمَلُوا۟ صَـٰلِحًا (And act righteously). إِنِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ (I have always, in regards to everything that you do, had full knowledge).

This is remarkable - Allah ﷻ takes messengers from across generations, and because He transcends time, He addresses all those messengers simultaneously in this ayah. “Messengers, eat and consume from the good and pure things, and act righteously. I no doubt have always had full knowledge of everything you do.”

Ayah 52

وَإِنَّ هَ

Ayah 54–118 · linked video: 03. Al-Mu’minun (Ayah 54-118)

Ayah 54

فَذَرْهُمْ فِى غَمْرَتِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ

Leave them in their unconsciousness until their time comes. This divine instruction addresses the reality of dealing with those who have become deeply entrenched in their deviation. These people have broken themselves into competing groups and factions, each content with their own version of truth, and Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ to leave them in the depths of their delusion.

The word غَمْرَة refers to the depths of water, painting a picture of people drowning in unconsciousness, too far gone to recognize the truth even when it’s presented to them. This presents a powerful principle for understanding how to approach religious deviations and factions. Islam is no different from other religions in experiencing the emergence of new ideas, variations and interpretations. The Prophet ﷺ even warned us about the fracturing of the Ummah into numerous groups.

Our instinctive response might be to identify every deviant group, catalogue their errors and systematically refute each one. But this approach has a fundamental flaw: while you’re busy dismantling one faction, others emerge. You become so consumed with addressing incorrect religion that you fail to teach the correct one. The prophets themselves demonstrated a more practical approach: address the major problems of your society and stick firmly to the core revelation. Teach the authentic teachings of the religion consistently and clearly.

When you make the correct understanding commonplace, something beautiful happens. People who have goodness in their hearts, regardless of which faction they currently belong to, will find what they’ve been missing. They’ll discover the authentic teachings and Allah ﷻ will guide them back to the correct path. Our primary responsibility is to make the proper understanding accessible and widespread, and Allah ﷻ will draw people of sincere hearts toward it.

We must also recognize that not everyone belonging to a particular group or holding a specific inclination is necessarily deviant. Many legitimate and valuable scholars, schools of thought and organizations exist within the broad spectrum of Islamic understanding. We shouldn’t rush to judgment about them.

Ayah 55-56

أَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّمَا نُمِدُّهُم بِهِۦ مِن مَّالٍ وَبَنِينَ • نُسَارِعُ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ بَل لَّا يَشْعُرُونَ

Do they assume that because We extend to them wealth and children, We are rushing to bring them good things? Rather, they don’t even realize the truth of their situation. This addresses a dangerous delusion that afflicts religious cults and deviant groups whose primary concern becomes purely worldly. Their conversations revolve entirely around wealth, status and material success. Discussion of the afterlife vanishes from their discourse.

Allah ﷻ asks whether they think the worldly provisions they receive indicate divine approval or blessing. Do they believe We’re hastening good opportunities toward them? The reality couldn’t be more different. These apparent blessings might actually be a form of gradual punishment, giving them rope to hang themselves. They remain completely unaware of the actual spiritual danger they face. So many opportunities exist for them to return to the truth, yet they fail to recognize even one.

Ayah 57

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ هُم مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ رَبِّهِم مُّشْفِقُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ shifts to describing the characteristics of true believers, offering a stark contrast. Those who, from the fear of their Master, are truly shaken and terrified. The word مُشْفِقُونَ (mushfiqoon) conveys being overcome with fear, being deeply moved by consciousness of Allah’s ﷻ majesty and justice. This wholesome fear doesn’t paralyze but rather motivates righteous action.

Ayah 58

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ

And those who truly believe in the miraculous signs of their Master. Here’s where the solution to division emerges: the Quran itself becomes the unifier. Let the various factions remain fractured, but what can bring people back together? The Quran will. When conversation about the Quran becomes commonplace in our communities, it automatically begins unifying people.

Consider the practical evidence: even Muslims from vastly different factions unite around Quranic recitation. Everyone participates in tafsir classes regardless of their school of thought. When Arabic classes are offered to better understand the Quran, students from various backgrounds sit together. Why? Because they all want to learn more about Allah’s ﷻ Book. The Quran serves as a natural unifier across boundaries.

The phrase “they believe in the ayat of their Master” carries deeper meaning. They don’t just believe the ayat exist or acknowledge them intellectually. They have faith in the power of the ayat themselves, confidence that these verses can actually bring about unity in society. They trust in what the Quran can accomplish when given its proper place.

Ayah 59

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِرَبِّهِمْ لَا يُشْرِكُونَ

And those who never, ever commit shirk with their Master. The emphasis through repetition (لَا يُشْرِكُونَ) underscores absolute rejection of associating partners with Allah ﷻ in any form. True believers maintain pure monotheism without compromise.

Ayah 60

وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْتُونَ مَآ ءَاتَوا۟ وَّقُلُوبُهُمْ وَجِلَةٌ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ رَٰجِعُونَ

And those who give whatever they can give, while their hearts tremble with fear that they will return to their Master. They contribute what they’re able to contribute, whether in charity, good deeds or service, yet despite their efforts, their hearts remain filled with fear. The word وَجِلَة describes deep internal fear, the kind that makes the hair on your skin rise. Even as they perform righteous actions, they fear these deeds won’t be sufficient, that they’ll still be held accountable when they return to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 61

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَهُمْ لَهَا سَـٰبِقُونَ

Those are the ones racing toward good deeds, and they are the ones who will reach them first. Not only do they rush toward righteousness, they’re going to win the race. The word سَـٰبِقُونَ refers to those who arrive first, who outpace all competitors. This echoes the opening description of successful believers in this surah, creating thematic unity.

Ayah 62

وَلَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۖ وَلَدَيْنَا كِتَـٰبٌ يَنطِقُ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ

We never burden any person beyond their capacity. This reassurance addresses a concern that might arise from the demanding characteristics just described. Allah ﷻ doesn’t require what’s impossible. Each person is held accountable only for what lies within their capability.

And We have a book in Our possession that speaks the truth. Books don’t literally speak, yet on the Day of Judgment, each person’s record will actually testify. The book of deeds will vocally declare what occurred. When that happens, they will not be wronged in the slightest. The speaking record ensures perfect justice.

Ayah 63

بَلْ قُلُوبُهُمْ فِى غَمْرَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا وَلَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلٌ مِّن دُونِ ذَٰلِكَ هُمْ لَهَا عَـٰمِلُونَ

Rather, their hearts exist in deep unconsciousness regarding this message. The word غَمْرَة returns, that same drowning in depths of delusion. Their hearts remain profoundly unconscious because of their worldly preoccupations. They’re too busy with material concerns to contemplate revelation.

And they have deeds other than this that occupy them. They’re preoccupied with activities completely unrelated to what matters. They have entertainment to consume, games to watch, shows to binge. They claim no time for Quran. This small phrase captures the lifestyle of most people you encounter: they work so they can play, then play until they must work again, then work to earn money for more play. An endless cycle. That unconscious state, that perpetual loop of work and play, defines their entire existence.

Ayah 64

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَخَذْنَا مُتْرَفِيهِم بِٱلْعَذَابِ إِذَا هُمْ يَجْـَٔرُونَ

Until We seize the elite among them with punishment, then suddenly they’re screaming. Some people remain stuck in that work-play cycle forever. But others succeed tremendously in worldly terms, becoming very wealthy and achieving high social status. When they reach that pinnacle of material success, that’s precisely when We grab them with punishment. And immediately they’re crying out like wild animals caught by a predator. The word يَجْـَٔرُونَ describes the cry a deer makes when seized by a wolf, or a seal when attacked by a shark—that primal scream of terror and pain.

Ayah 65

لَا تَجْـَٔرُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ إِنَّكُم مِّنَّا لَا تُنصَرُونَ

Don’t cry out like animals today. No help is coming to you from Us. You’re acting as though you’re the victim, crying out for rescue. But you’re not the victim—you’re the criminal. This reversal of roles, where the guilty party claims victimhood, receives sharp divine rebuke.

Ayah 66

قَدْ كَانَتْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَكُنتُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَـٰبِكُمْ تَنكِصُونَ

My miraculous signs were being recited to you constantly, yet you used to turn back on your heels. The image is vivid: you’re walking forward when suddenly someone ahead starts reciting Quran or discussing Islam. You immediately spin around and retreat, fleeing the other direction. تَنكِصُونَ means to beat a hasty retreat, to turn away from opportunities for good. “They’re talking about Quran over there—let’s go somewhere else.” “I don’t want to visit that house, they always have a reminder after salah.” This avoidance, this active fleeing from religious discourse, characterized their behavior.

Ayah 67

مُسْتَكْبِرِينَ بِهِۦ سَـٰمِرًا تَهْجُرُونَ

You were arrogant because of it, spending nights telling inappropriate stories. There’s an interesting grammatical shift here: مُسْتَكْبِرِينَ (mustakbireen) but then سَـٰمِرًا appears in singular form when it should match in plurality. This draws attention to their behavior. سَامِر refers to staying up at night telling stories, conversing into the late hours.

They were arrogant specifically because of the Sacred House. The Quraysh served as custodians of the Kaaba and used that position to justify their superiority. “Why should we listen to anyone else? This House belongs to us. Our nation maintains it. Who are these people to tell us about its true purpose?” This arrogance stemmed from their custodianship.

All night they would sit around the Kaaba—imagine this violation—telling stories, getting intoxicated, engaging in all manner of inappropriate behavior right beside the House of Allah ﷻ. For centuries the Kaaba was desecrated this way. Allah ﷻ blessed them with so much, and they responded with such defiance. This reminds me of poetry that describes teaching someone archery daily until their arm grows strong enough to pull the bow fully back, then they shoot you with it. Or teaching someone the craft of poetry, and when they finally compose verses, they use them to criticize their teacher. Similarly, Allah ﷻ gave these people everything—a messenger, revelation, the Sacred House—and they used it all to defy Him.

تَهْجُرُونَ means to speak improperly, to say things that shouldn’t be said, to migrate away from appropriate speech. Notice even today this phenomenon persists: people gather late at night just talking, and the later the hour gets, the more inappropriate their conversation becomes. Guards drop. Someone says something questionable, then someone else shares a crude joke, then another, and the discourse degenerates.

Ayah 68

أَفَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا۟ ٱلْقَوْلَ أَمْ جَآءَهُم مَّا لَمْ يَأْتِ ءَابَآءَهُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ
