Haven’t they reflected on this speech at all? Every instance of the Quran being shared is described as قَوْل (qawl)—conversation, speech. Haven’t they contemplated this conversation that’s reaching them? Or have they received something their earliest ancestors never heard, giving them legitimate reason to reject it? This echoes criticisms made by the people of Nuh (AS) and other ancient peoples who claimed, “We never heard this from our forefathers.”

Ayah 69

أَمْ لَمْ يَعْرِفُوا۟ رَسُولَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ

Or is it that they didn’t recognize their messenger, so they find him strange and reject him? Did they not know who this person is? Were they unaware of his character, his honesty, his truthfulness? Is their justification that they simply don’t know him? They’re acting completely baffled by him when they know exactly who he is.

Ayah 70

أَمْ يَقُولُونَ بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌۢ ۚ بَلْ جَآءَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كَـٰرِهُونَ

Or do they claim he’s possessed by jinn? Rather, he has come to them with the absolute truth, but most of them simply dislike the truth. I have a friend who researches genetics, and his coworkers also conduct genetic research. Sometimes he discusses Islam with them, and usually they respond with philosophical objections. One colleague he regularly has lunch with, he finally asked directly: “You study genetics. Can you honestly tell me to my face that there’s no God after everything you’ve researched?” The colleague admitted, “No, I can’t say that.” “Then why don’t you even consider Islam?” The response: “Because I don’t want to change my lifestyle. I like how I live.”

After all the intellectual arguments dissolve, what remains underneath? “I just don’t want to live differently than I’m living. I’m content as I am.” Most of them dislike the truth not because of rational objections but because accepting it would require changing how they live.

Ayah 71

وَلَوِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلْحَقُّ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ لَفَسَدَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهِنَّ ۚ بَلْ أَتَيْنَـٰهُم بِذِكْرِهِمْ فَهُمْ عَن ذِكْرِهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ

If the truth were to follow their desires, the heavens and earth would have been corrupted, along with everyone within them. This addresses something critically important in our times. Contemporary culture tells us “the truth is whatever you want it to be,” “What is your truth?” “Do what makes you feel good.” Even religious advice gets filtered through this lens: “Follow your religion if it makes you feel good, but if it doesn’t make you feel good, don’t follow it.”

Imagine someone tells a psychiatrist, “I’m having thoughts I know are forbidden in my religion. These thoughts keep occurring.” The psychiatrist responds, “If it makes you feel good, you shouldn’t feel guilty about it.” The truth should follow you rather than you following the truth. If something makes you feel guilty, just stop feeling guilty and embrace it. This represents a complete inversion of reality, which is why we desperately need Islamic psychiatry and counseling. Modern psychology often pushes people further from truth.

Even church groups conducting family counseling fall into this trap. A husband admits he’s no longer attracted to his wife and frequents questionable establishments. The counselor’s advice? “Why don’t you watch inappropriate content at home instead—it’s safer.” This is their solution. The world needs true counsel that points toward truth rather than bending truth to accommodate desires.

If the truth had to bend to their whims, corruption would spread through the skies and earth, and everyone within them would be affected. Rather, We have brought them their own reminder—ذِكْر, their own mention, directly relevant to them. Yet regarding their own reminder, they turn away.

This part is crucial because when someone receives advice, they often deflect: “I don’t want to change, and besides, this doesn’t relate to me. You’re talking about a different kind of person. I’m unique. This Quranic advice isn’t about me—it’s about someone else.” Allah ﷻ says We brought them their own mention, their own specific guidance, and it’s precisely their own guidance they’re denying. It speaks directly about them, and they know it.

Ayah 72

أَمْ تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ خَرْجًا فَخَرَاجُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ

Are you asking them for some payment? The provision from your Master is far better, and He is the best of all providers. The Arabic uses two forms here: خَرْجًا and خَرَاجُ. When they might give financial support, the lighter word is used. When Allah’s ﷻ provision is mentioned, the weightier, more powerful form appears—even the vocabulary emphasizes that His provision vastly exceeds any human offering.

The messenger is being told: don’t think Islam needs those wealthy individuals criticized throughout this surah. Don’t imagine that if one of those billionaires embraced Islam, suddenly everything would improve and countless masajid could be built. Is that what you want from them? What Allah ﷻ provides is infinitely better.

Ayah 73

وَإِنَّكَ لَتَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

You have no other agenda whatsoever. You’re not seeking their wealth. You’re not pursuing personal gain. You are truly, genuinely inviting them to a straight path. That’s your sole objective.

Ayah 74

وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَنِ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ لَنَـٰكِبُونَ

And those who refuse to believe in the afterlife—when it comes to the path, they constantly deviate. نَـٰكِبُونَ means to lower your head and walk away, to tip over like a spilling cup, to blow sideways like wind that changes direction. They deviate, sidestep and change the subject. Every time conversation turns toward the true path, they redirect it. They introduce distractions. They refuse to engage with what matters most.

Ayah 75

وَلَوْ رَحِمْنَـٰهُمْ وَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِم مِّن ضُرٍّ لَّلَجُّوا۟ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ

Even if We showed them mercy and removed whatever difficulty afflicted them, they would persist stubbornly in their rebellion, blindly wandering. Throughout history, when messengers came, Allah ﷻ often brought difficulty to that land—trials designed to soften hearts. The purpose was making people more receptive to the messenger’s call, humbling them so they’d listen. Makkah experienced severe famine during the Prophet’s ﷺ time.

Allah ﷻ says even if We showed them mercy and lifted all their difficulties, these particular people would have remained insistent upon rebellion. This reveals something profound: the Messenger ﷺ faced the toughest audience any prophet ever encountered. No generation received a harder test. Any normal group of people, when subjected to hardship, would turn back to Allah ﷻ. But these people? Even if difficulties were removed, they’d persist in defiance, remaining blind of heart. The word عَمَه means blindness of the heart, distinct from physical blindness.

Ayah 76

وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْعَذَابِ فَمَا ٱسْتَكَانُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمْ وَمَا يَتَضَرَّعُونَ

We already seized them with punishment, yet they didn’t humble themselves before their Master, nor did they supplicate humbly. This references both the immediate audience and previous nations. Why were earlier peoples seized with punishment? Because they wouldn’t humble themselves. They continued rebelling and refused to become humble, so destruction came and annihilated them completely.

Ayah 77

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَابًا ذَا عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ إِذَا هُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ

Until when We open against them a gate of severe punishment, suddenly they’re in complete despair. When that door opens—when the floodgates of extreme punishment unleash—all hope vanishes instantly. مُبْلِسُونَ describes utter hopelessness, absolute desperation with no way out.

Ayah 78

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ

He is the One who created for you hearing, sight and hearts—yet how little you show gratitude. The word أَفْـِٔدَة refers to hearts, but not just as emotional organs. Some linguists argue this specifically means sound intellect, strong minds capable of making proper decisions. It encompasses both emotional hearts and intellectual capacity. Despite being granted these incredible faculties, how rarely people express genuine thankfulness.

Ayah 79

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى ذَرَأَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ

He is the One who spread you throughout the earth, and to Him you will be gathered. The verb ذَرَأَ describes how farmers scatter seeds across a field. Allah ﷻ scattered humanity across the earth in similar fashion, and just as those seeds will eventually be collected, so too will all people be gathered back to Him.

Ayah 80

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ وَلَهُ ٱخْتِلَـٰفُ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

He is the One who gives life and causes death, and to Him belongs the alternation of night and day. This doesn’t happen automatically or by natural law—He actively owns and controls the conflict between darkness and light. He makes each night happen, He brings each day into being. Then why don’t you apply your intellect? Why don’t you engage your reasoning?

Ayah 81

بَلْ قَالُوا۟ مِثْلَ مَا قَالَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ

Rather, they said exactly what the earliest ones said. This comment carries particular weight because of what we’ve learned throughout this surah. Nuh (AS) came and his people responded with certain objections. Then another generation arose and said the exact same thing. Then more generations followed, repeating identical arguments. Now we’re dealing with the people of Makkah, and Allah ﷻ says they’ve said precisely what all previous nations said.

But the lesson doesn’t stop at noting the repetition. Those previous generations weren’t just ignored—they were destroyed for making these statements. So when Allah ﷻ says the Quraysh are repeating what earlier peoples said, the implication is clear: they should expect to be dealt with no differently than those who came before.

Ayah 82

قَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ

They said, “When we die and become dust and bones, will we really be raised again?” The same question recycled through generations. The elite used to mockingly ask, “You really believe you’ll come back to life after death?”

Ayah 83

لَقَدْ وُعِدْنَا نَحْنُ وَءَابَآؤُنَا هَـٰذَا مِن قَبْلُ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

This was promised to us before, and to our ancestors before us—this is nothing but legends of the ancients. For the first time in the Quran, the Quraysh actually admit something significant. They acknowledge that in their historical legacy exists some discourse about the afterlife. “We’ve heard these things before. Somebody mentioned—maybe our grandfather talked about the legacy of Ibrahim (AS), about resurrection, about the next life.” They’re admitting previous awareness existed, but dismissing it as أَسَـٰطِيرُ—ancient legends and folk tales.

The word أَسَاطِير comes from a Greek origin and eventually traveled into English as “story.” They’re claiming these teachings amount to old stories, fictional tales passed down through generations without substance or truth.

Ayah 84-85

قُل لِّمَنِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ • سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ ۚ قُلْ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ

Ask them: who does the earth belong to, and whoever is on it, if you truly know? They’re going to respond that it belongs to Allah. They’ll admit readily, “Sure, it’s Allah’s ﷻ earth.” Then ask: why don’t you make any effort to remember Him? Why don’t you take proper advice from this acknowledgment?

Ayah 86-87

قُلْ مَن رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ ٱلسَّبْعِ وَرَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ • سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ ۚ قُلْ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

Ask them: who is the Master of the seven heavens and Master of the Mighty Throne? They’ll still respond, “Allah.” Then ask: why aren’t you taking any precaution? Why aren’t you protecting yourselves from His punishment when you claim to believe in His ultimate authority?

Ayah 88-89

قُلْ مَنۢ بِيَدِهِۦ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ يُجِيرُ وَلَا يُجَارُ عَلَيْهِ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ • سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ ۚ قُلْ فَأَنَّىٰ تُسْحَرُونَ

Ask them: in whose hand is the dominion of everything, and He is the One who offers refuge while no one can offer refuge against Him—if you truly know? The word يُجِيرُ comes from جَار, meaning neighbor. It refers to offering protection to those close to you, bringing them under your safeguard. Allah ﷻ alone offers true refuge, and absolutely no one can provide protection against Him.

They’ll respond, “Allah.” Then ask: how are you so mystified? How are you taken in by magic and delusion when you acknowledge all this?

Ayah 90

بَلْ أَتَيْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ

Rather, the fact is We brought them the truth, and they are lying. Every time they answered “Allah” to those questions, they were lying. Not lying about Allah’s ﷻ existence or attributes—they genuinely believe Allah is the Master, the Owner, the Ultimate Authority. But their hearts remain in denial. Their intellectual acknowledgment doesn’t translate into submission or obedience.

This reveals something crucial that philosophy students should understand: simply believing in God isn’t enough. Why isn’t it sufficient? Because believing in God doesn’t automatically mean you know what to believe about God. You develop your own ideas about who He is, what He wants from you, what He’s like, why He created you. You answer these questions independently based on your imagination and preferences.

When you craft your own concept of God, the truth is following you. If you truly believe in God, you cannot do so properly without revelation. Only God Himself can tell you what He wants from you, who He actually is, what His qualities are, what His teachings encompass, why you were placed on earth, where you’re heading. Those questions can’t be answered by human speculation. You must follow the truth rather than making truth follow you.

That’s why after these questions about Allah’s ﷻ attributes, He doesn’t say “Let me tell you who Allah is”—because they already know who Allah is in basic terms. What they need to know is what He expects, and that expectation is the truth they must submit to.

Ayah 91

مَا ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِن وَلَدٍ وَمَا كَانَ مَعَهُۥ مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ ۚ إِذًا لَّذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلَـٰهٍۭ بِمَا خَلَقَ وَلَعَلَا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ

When people develop their own concepts of divinity, it becomes easy to claim Allah has a son. So Allah ﷻ declares: Allah never took a son whatsoever, and there are absolutely no associated gods with Him. The same people who correctly answered “Allah” to every question about divine authority also claimed Allah has daughters and sons represented by the idols they worship.

If multiple gods existed, each would have fought with the others. Think about what happens when people have a dispute and someone moves out—they take all their belongings. If multiple deities existed, each would claim their creations: “I made the mountains, I’m taking those.” “I created the seas, those are mine.” “I made human heads, I’m keeping the heads.” They would fight one another, attempting to establish supremacy and overpower the others.

This is exactly what fills mythology—gods battling each other, using their respective creations as weapons in cosmic conflicts. How far above such descriptions is Allah ﷻ! How elevated beyond these artistic depictions, these elaborate mythological tales they’ve invented!

Ayah 92

عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

The Knower of the unseen and the seen—He is far above all the associations they make. This is a general statement about Allah’s ﷻ knowledge, but its placement here carries specific significance. People who want to develop their own beliefs about Allah—claiming He has a son, has subordinate deities, has various partners—are making assertions about the unseen realm. Even according to their own theology, these claims about divine nature exist beyond visible reality.

They’re inventing concepts from creative imagination, projecting human experiences and relationships onto the divine. Allah ﷻ is the only One who actually knows what lies beyond what you can see with your own eyes. You have no right to fabricate stories about things you haven’t seen and cannot know. That knowledge can only come from Allah ﷻ through revelation. He alone knows the unseen and the seen, and He is completely above the false associations they attribute to Him.

Ayah 93-94

قُل رَّبِّ إِمَّا تُرِيَنِّى مَا يُوعَدُونَ • رَبِّ فَلَا تَجْعَلْنِى فِى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

The Messenger ﷺ is instructed to say: Master, if You would just show me what they’ve been promised—if You would give me even a glimpse of the punishment coming their way. And Allah ﷻ actually fulfilled this prayer on the occasion of Isra and Mi’raj. He showed the Prophet ﷺ a glimpse of Hellfire, allowed him to see people being punished.

Then don’t place me among the wrongdoing nation. If Allah ﷻ were to show you the consequences of their disbelief, the punishments being held back like a restrained animal—think about the previous passage where we learned Allah ﷻ holds the sky back from crashing down, prevents meteor showers from striking, constantly protects this planet. If He simply revealed to you, Messenger, the scale of punishment being restrained, you would immediately pray: Master, don’t keep me with these people. Don’t let me be associated with this wrongdoing nation.

We learned in Surah al-Anfal that believers should fear a punishment that won’t exclusively target wrongdoers. When Allah’s ﷻ punishment descends, often entire populations face annihilation. So even getting a glimpse of what’s being held back would make the Messenger ﷺ urgently request separation from that nation.

Ayah 95

وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّرِيَكَ مَا نَعِدُهُمْ لَقَـٰدِرُونَ

We are completely capable of showing you what We’ve promised them. We have full ability to give you that glimpse, to thoroughly display what awaits them.

Ayah 96

ٱدْفَعْ بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ ۚ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَصِفُونَ

Continue to repel evil with what is better. People will direct insulting words at you. They’ll say offensive things about Islam. They’ll engage in سَامِر—spending nights talking nonsense. Remember the previous surah mentioned someone called Sameer? That name stuck to him because of his constant nonsense talk.

When you hear this kind of nonsense, respond with the better word. Find the superior response. If they resort to insults, identify the only reasonable element in their statement and address that without descending to insults yourself. Maintain the moral high ground. We know better the kinds of descriptions and accusations they make—you don’t need to match their level.

Ayah 97-98

وَقُل رَّبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ هَمَزَٰتِ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ • وَأَعُوذُ بِكَ رَبِّ أَن يَحْضُرُونِ

Say: Master, I seek Your refuge from the whispers and prodding of the devils. I seek Your refuge that they should ever be present before me. The prophets are protected from being influenced by Shaytan—that’s guaranteed. But being influenced and being disturbed are two different things.

Imagine a kid sitting next to you continuously poking you with his pen. Poke, poke, poke. You have the ability to not let it affect your work, to not turn around and strike him. You maintain composure and continue what you’re doing even as he persists. The prophets possess special ability to withstand that constant poking and prodding from Shaytan. The effects get cancelled out. We learned in the previous surah about هَمَز—that persistent poking—and how Allah ﷻ neutralizes its impact for the prophets.

Now the Messenger ﷺ is told to ask for protection from their continual harassment. The devils don’t give up easily. Even the Messenger ﷺ must request this protection because his ability isn’t self-generated—it’s granted by Allah ﷻ. So he asks Allah ﷻ to maintain that protective ability.

He goes further: not just protection from their poking, but I seek Your refuge that they should never come before me at all. Give me security detail from angels so that the moment Shaytan approaches the neighborhood, they intervene and keep them away. Grant me that level of protection.

By extension, this prayer the Prophet ﷺ made extends to all of us. We should similarly seek Allah’s ﷻ protection from the influence and presence of devils.

Ayah 99-100

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَحَدَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ قَالَ رَبِّ ٱرْجِعُونِ • لَعَلِّىٓ أَعْمَلُ صَـٰلِحًا فِيمَا تَرَكْتُ ۚ كَلَّآ ۚ إِنَّهَا كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قَآئِلُهَا ۖ وَمِن وَرَآئِهِم بَرْزَخٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ

The Messenger ﷺ should ask for protection from Shaytan, but look at these people—they won’t ask to return to Allah ﷻ until death itself arrives. Only at that very moment do they say, “Master, send me back. Hopefully I’ll act righteously, take advantage of everything I left behind, do all the good deeds I abandoned.”

كَلَّا —No way. Absolutely not. إِنَّهَا كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قَآئِلُهَا—This is just words he’s uttering, just running his mouth. Allah ﷻ already knows exactly what they would do. Earlier He said if We lifted punishment away, they’d persist in deviation. Now someone stands before Allah ﷻ on their deathbed claiming they’d be different, but they wouldn’t.

Think about documentaries showing drug addicts, gang members who get caught and appear before a juvenile judge. They promise they won’t repeat their crimes. What happens? They do it again, and again, and again. They’re stuck in that life, and the experienced judge recognizes the pattern. “I know how this goes.”

There’s a fascinating book that recently came out about ending juvenile delinquency in America, written by a Brooklyn juvenile court judge who served for forty years. His conclusion after seeing generations of youth fall into crime? Fathers need to act like fathers. Too many single-parent homes lack father figures, and that absence leads youth into criminal lives. Even homes with fathers often have men who don’t fulfill that role properly, and that’s where juvenile delinquency originates.

كَلَّا—This is just something he’s saying. وَمِن وَرَآئِهِم بَرْزَخٌ—And right behind them stands a huge barrier. This introduces the concept of بَرْزَخ—the life of the grave. The word literally means a barrier between two things. There’s this life, there’s the afterlife, and the gap between represents a very different existence. It’s a significant divide, a major difference from worldly life, lasting until the Day they’re raised.

Ayah 101

فَإِذَا نُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَلَآ أَنسَابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَلَا يَتَسَآءَلُونَ

When the trumpet is blown, there will be no relationships between them that day, nor will they ask about one another. أَنسَاب describes relationships, the connections people draw. “You know that person?” “Yeah, that’s my uncle.” “That’s my cousin.” “That’s my friend.”

My sister does this constantly. She’ll be at someone’s house and they’ll mention me, and she’ll say, “Yeah, that’s my brother.” They respond, “Yeah, we know, he’s everybody’s brother.” She insists, “No, that’s actually my brother.” It becomes a running joke.

The idea of claiming relationship, drawing closeness through association—that completely vanishes. Judgment Day arrives and people who used to form tight groups, the elite who socialized together, attended all the same parties, those relationships evaporate. فَلَآ أَنسَابَ بَيْنَهُمْ—No relationships exist between them that day. وَلَا يَتَسَآءَلُونَ—Nor do they even ask about one another. At a party, you notice someone missing: “Hey, where’s Mike? Sam didn’t show up—anyone know where he went?” Nobody’s asking. Nobody misses anybody. No concern for who’s present or absent.

Contrast this with what we’ll learn about the people of Jannah. You arrive at that gathering and realize, “Wait, my friend isn’t here.” You ask about him, inquire what happened, and discover he ended up in Hellfire. At least you cared enough to ask. But when the trumpet blows for these people, they won’t even ask about anyone else.

Ayah 102

فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

Those whose scales are heavy—those are the ultimately successful. Notice the connection: ultimate success was mentioned at the beginning of this surah with قَدْ أَفْلَحَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ. The surah ends where it began, tying lessons together in a complete circle.

Something important about the language here: there’s a difference between saying “scales that are heavy” versus “scales that were made heavy.” The passive construction “were made heavy” implies an external force acted upon them. But the text says ثَقُلَتْ—they became heavy on their own merits. These people’s deeds possess inherent value. Their actions are genuinely worthy in themselves.

Ayah 103

وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فِى جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدُونَ

As for those whose scales are light—those are the ones who bankrupted themselves, remaining permanently in Jahannam. Again, خَفَّتْ means they became light on their own. Allah ﷻ didn’t arbitrarily render their deeds worthless. Their deeds were worthless from the beginning, based on their own characteristics. That’s why they weigh nothing—on their own lack of merit.

Those are the ones who truly bankrupted themselves, threw themselves into loss. فِى جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدُونَ—In Jahannam they will remain permanently.

Ayah 104

تَلْفَحُ وُجُوهَهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَهُمْ فِيهَا كَـٰلِحُونَ

The fire will blister their faces, and in it they will have distorted expressions. Their skin becomes twisted and discolored from the flames. كَـٰلِحُونَ describes an ugly, contorted face—the expression someone makes during torture. The word also indicates lips that can’t touch one another, a mouth forced open from continuous crying and screaming. The anguish is so intense they remain frozen in that horrified expression, unable even to close their mouths. خَـٰلِدُونَ—Permanently in that state.

You should keep something in mind about this description. Remember earlier in the surah we learned about people who spent nights running their mouths, talking endlessly against the Prophet ﷺ and against the Quran? سَـٰمِرًا تَهْجُرُونَ—Spending nights speaking inappropriately, unable to stop talking. Now in Jahannam? Keep your mouth open. You wanted to keep talking, keep your lips open—but this time from torture.

Ayah 105

أَلَمْ تَكُنْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَكُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ

Weren’t My ayat constantly recited to you, yet you used to deny them? When the ayat are recited, what should a believer do? The Quran gives instruction: أَنصِتُوا—stop talking and listen. Two essential actions: stop talking, and listen. Weren’t My ayat the ones being recited to you all the time? And you were the people who used to lie against them, who used to deny them.

Ayah 106

قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا غَلَبَتْ عَلَيْنَا شِقْوَتُنَا وَكُنَّا قَوْمًا ضَآلِّينَ

They said: Our Master, our wretchedness overcame us—our bad luck, our evil side, our dark side. The Force within us wasn’t strong enough. That’s their excuse. وَكُنَّا قَوْمًا ضَآلِّينَ—We were just lost people, misled, confused.

Ayah 107

رَبَّنَآ أَخْرِجْنَا مِنْهَا فَإِنْ عُدْنَا فَإِنَّا ظَـٰلِمُونَ

Our Master, extract us from this place. Please get us out of here. فَإِنْ عُدْنَا فَإِنَّا ظَـٰلِمُونَ—If we return to our previous behavior, then You can declare us criminals. Give us a second chance. If we mess up again, fine, we admit we’re wrongdoers. But please, one more opportunity. It’s like a student negotiating: “Can I retake this exam? If I fail the makeup, I totally deserve whatever grade you give me.”

Ayah 108

قَالَ ٱخْسَـُٔوا۟ فِيهَا وَلَا تُكَلِّمُونِ

Allah ﷻ says: اخْسَـُٔوا فِيهَا—Be gone into it. Get lost like animals. When the Israelites were turned into monkeys for violating the Sabbath, they were told اخْسَـُٔوا—be shooed away. This word is used for dogs, pigs, monkeys and other animals you don’t want near you. Get away from here like animals being pushed away.

وَلَا تُكَلِّمُونِ—And don’t talk to Me. Don’t even speak to Me.

Ayah 109

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْ عِبَادِى يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ

There used to be a group among My slaves who would say: “Our Master, we believe, so forgive us and show us mercy—You are the best of those who show mercy.”

Now you want to say رَبَّنَا? This isn’t the time for that. That was the time—back when you were alive. إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْ عِبَادِى—There was a group of My slaves saying exactly these words. Where were you? You weren’t alone. You think nobody was calling on Allah ﷻ this way? My slaves were making these prayers constantly.

Yes, I am the Most Merciful. I was giving you mercy constantly. All you had to say were these very words you’re saying now—but you needed to say them then, when it mattered. Now you come asking for mercy? Now you want a second chance? I give endless chances. I’m الرَّحِيم. My believing slaves made mistakes and I forgave them, again and again and again. You’re asking like I never gave you opportunities, like this is your first request. It’s far too late.

Ayah 110

فَٱتَّخَذْتُمُوهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا حَتَّىٰ أَنسَوْكُمْ ذِكْرِى وَكُنتُم مِّنْهُمْ تَضْحَكُونَ

You took them as a joke, as something to mock. حَتَّىٰ أَنسَوْكُمْ ذِكْرِى—So much so that they made you forget My remembrance. You were so busy ridiculing the believers that you forgot about Me entirely. وَكُنتُم مِّنْهُمْ تَضْحَكُونَ—All of you used to laugh at them constantly, make fun of them all the time.

Ayah 111

إِنِّى جَزَيْتُهُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ بِمَا صَبَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ

Today I am compensating them because of their patience. What test of patience appears in this passage? The believers were being mocked, ridiculed and laughed at by disbelievers. Yet they continued calling on Allah ﷻ privately, persistently. They didn’t retaliate. They maintained their practice despite the mockery. Allah ﷻ says: I’m compensating them today. They’re getting paid. أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ—They are the ones who are ultimately successful.

الْفَآئِزُونَ is a powerful word related to فَوْز. When someone dies, you can say فَازَ الرَّجُلُ—the man died. How does death connect to success? Because فَوْز describes success in a matter where failure would have meant death. It’s not success in an exam you can survive failing. It’s success in climbing a mountain where not reaching the checkpoint would have killed you—success related to survival, where failure equals death but success brings enormous rewards. That’s the definition of فَوْز.

They are the ones who truly attained that ultimate success.

Ayah 112-113

قَـٰلَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ • قَالُوا۟ لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ فَسْـَٔلِ ٱلْعَآدِّينَ

He asks them: How many years did you remain on earth? Give Me a count of years. Both believers and disbelievers respond the same way: We must have lived a day, or maybe part of a day. We don’t think we lived any longer. فَسْـَٔلِ ٱلْعَآدِّينَ—Ask those who keep count. Ask the angels who constantly recorded our deeds, marking every day, every hour, every minute of what we did.

Ayah 114

قَـٰلَ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا ۖ لَّوْ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

He says: You remained only a little while—if only you had known. Even though they lived many years, it doesn’t amount to much. You were there for very little. لَّوْ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ—If you really understood this reality.

In other words, the time you and I have on this earth, Allah ﷻ describes as very little. We should have a sense of urgency about it. Think of it like a single day, because when you look back, you’re going to call it that—a day, or part of a day.

Ayah 115

أَفَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّمَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ عَبَثًا وَأَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْنَا لَا تُرْجَعُونَ

Did you think We created you without purpose? عَبَثًا describes something purposeless, a meaningful task treated as trivial, considered worthless and unimportant. When do you act like you don’t have purpose? When you think you have unlimited free time. “What are you doing?” “Nothing.” Just sitting around. The couch potato lifestyle.

Did you think We created you just to play around, just to chill? وَأَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْنَا لَا تُرْجَعُونَ—And that you wouldn’t be returned to Us?

Ayah 116

فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْكَرِيمِ

How exalted is Allah ﷻ, the True King, high above such allegations. لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ—No one deserves worship and obedience except Him. رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْكَرِيمِ—Master of the Noble Throne.

Ayah 117

وَمَن يَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ لَا بُرْهَـٰنَ لَهُۥ بِهِۦ فَإِنَّمَا حِسَابُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ

Whoever calls upon any god besides Allah ﷻ—and there is absolutely no proof, no case to be made for such worship—his reckoning will be with his Master. إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ—Disbelievers will never, ever be successful. They will not attain success.

Ayah 118

وَقُل رَّبِّ ٱغْفِرْ وَٱرْحَمْ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ

And say: Master, forgive and show mercy—You are the best of those who show mercy. This is such a beautiful ending. We just witnessed people on Judgment Day begging Allah ﷻ for mercy, and He told them: Don’t ask now. There were people who used to ask properly, at the right time.

At the conclusion of everything, the Messenger ﷺ is instructed to lead by example, teaching people how to ask: رَّبِّ ٱغْفِرْ وَٱرْحَمْ—Master, forgive and show mercy.

There’s a subtle but crucial distinction here. Jannah is not merely the consequence of forgiveness—Jannah is the consequence of Allah’s ﷻ mercy. Not being punished represents forgiveness. If you’re not burning in Hellfire, that means you’re forgiven. But that doesn’t automatically mean Allah ﷻ has shown you mercy, because mercy goes beyond not punishing. Mercy is the good added on top.

Just because someone isn’t in Hellfire doesn’t mean they’ve earned Jannah. It just means they’re not a criminal. Just because you’re not bankrupt doesn’t mean you’re a millionaire—it just means you’re not bankrupt. Allah ﷻ doesn’t only forgive, which would merely place you at zero, safe from Hellfire. He then shows mercy, meaning He enters you into Paradise.

Think of Hellfire as negative and Paradise as positive. Being forgiven means you’re at zero—the negatives are removed. But the surah began by promising Jannah, promising the positive. قَدْ أَفْلَحَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ—The believers have succeeded, achieved the good. So the surah ends with رَّبِّ ٱغْفِرْ وَٱرْحَمْ—asking for both forgiveness and mercy. At minimum, forgive me so the account is clean, the negatives are gone, I’m at zero. Then show mercy, add blessings, multiply my good deeds, reward them.

وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ—You are the best of those who show mercy. الرَّٰحِمِينَ in Arabic refers to one who shows mercy immediately, who engages in acts of immediate mercy—like someone standing and showing mercy right now. Master, You are the best of those showing mercy even in this moment. Yes, You will grant me Jannah, but even now I don’t deny that You’re showing me mercy continually.

May Allah ﷻ help us appreciate and love His Book. بَارَكَ اللَّهُ لِي وَلَكُمْ فِي الْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ.
